passages.title: Earth's Seven Natural Wonders
passages.body:
Long ago, people made a list of the most amazing places on Earth and called them the Seven Natural Wonders. These places remind us how powerful and beautiful nature can be.

The list includes the Grand Canyon in the United States, Mount Everest in Asia, the Great Barrier Reef in Australia, the Northern Lights in the far north, Paricutin Volcano in Mexico, the Harbor of Rio de Janeiro in Brazil, and Victoria Falls in Africa. Each one was chosen because it is huge, rare, or breathtaking.

For example, Mount Everest is the tallest mountain in the world. Climbers train for years before they try to reach its summit. The Great Barrier Reef is so large that astronauts can see it from space, and it is home to thousands of kinds of fish. The Northern Lights paint the night sky with green and pink waves of light.

The Seven Natural Wonders show that our planet is full of surprises. They invite us to explore, to protect, and to feel small in the best way.

[Map: locations of the Seven Natural Wonders] [Photo caption: The Great Barrier Reef from above.]

questions.q: What is the central idea of this text?
choices: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
The Seven Natural Wonders show how amazing Earth is.
The Great Barrier Reef has many fish.
Astronauts can see Earth from space.

questions.q: Which detail SUPPORTS the idea that the wonders are huge or rare?
choices: People made a list long ago.
Climbers train for years to reach Everest's summit.
We should protect the planet.
The list has seven wonders.

questions.q: What does the [Map: locations of the Seven Natural Wonders] feature help readers do?
choices: Hear how the names sound
See where the wonders are around the world
Learn how tall Everest is
Count the wonders

questions.q: Which sentence is the BEST one-sentence summary?
choices: Mount Everest is in Asia.
Earth has seven famous natural places that show how powerful nature is.
The Northern Lights are green and pink.
Astronauts saw the reef from space.

questions.q: What is the topic of the text?
choices: Mountain climbing
The Seven Natural Wonders of Earth
Australia
Astronauts

passages.title: Down to the Deepest Place
passages.body:
First, scientists wanted to know what was at the bottom of the ocean. They knew the deepest spot was the Mariana Trench, but no one had ever been there. Next, they had a problem: the water near the bottom pushes down with the weight of an elephant on every fingertip. A normal submarine would be crushed.

So, engineers built a special submersible with very thick walls and tiny windows. They tested it carefully. Then, in 1960, two explorers climbed inside and rode it almost seven miles down into the trench. The trip took five hours.

When they finally landed on the bottom, they were surprised. They expected the trench to be empty. Instead, they saw a small fish swim past their window. Even in the darkest, coldest place on Earth, life had found a way.

Finally, what they learned changed science forever. Today, autonomous robots also visit the trench to take pictures and bring back samples, so we can keep learning about this amazing place.

questions.q: How is this text organized?
choices: Compare and contrast
Sequence (in time order)
Description only
Problem and solution only

questions.q: Which signal words tell you about the text structure?
choices: like, unlike, both
first, next, then, finally
for example, such as
because, so, since

questions.q: What problem did the engineers have to solve?
choices: The trench was too far away.
The water pressure would crush a normal submarine.
Nobody wanted to go.
The trench had no fish.

questions.q: Which sentence is the BEST summary?
choices: A fish lives in the trench.
Scientists built a special submersible to reach the deepest place on Earth and discovered life there.
The trip took five hours.
Robots take pictures.

questions.q: What happened AFTER the explorers landed on the bottom?
choices: They built the submersible.
They saw a fish swim by.
They tested the windows.
They wrote a book first.

passages.title: Weird and Wondrous Rocks
passages.body:
Rocks may look quiet and boring, but some rocks are as strange as anything in a story book. The Wave, in Arizona, swirls across the desert like ribbons of orange candy. Giant's Causeway, in Ireland, is a row of stone columns that fit together as neatly as honeycomb. People long ago thought a giant must have built it.

In Norway, a huge rock called Kjeragbolten is wedged between two cliffs, sitting in the air like a frozen marble. Brave hikers stand on top of it, while the wind whispers around them.

How did these wonders form? Patient nature is the artist. Wind, water, and ice slowly shape the stone for millions of years. A river can carve a canyon as easily as a knife slices cake — if you give it long enough.

The next time you pick up a plain pebble, remember: every rock has a long, secret story.

questions.q: Which sentence uses a SIMILE?
choices: Rocks may look quiet and boring.
The Wave swirls across the desert like ribbons of orange candy.
How did these wonders form?
Brave hikers stand on top of it.

questions.q: "The wind whispered around them" is an example of —
choices: a metaphor
personification
a hyperbole
an idiom

questions.q: What does the author mean by "Patient nature is the artist"?
choices: A real artist painted the rocks.
Nature slowly shapes the rocks like an artist makes art.
Artists are patient people.
Rocks are made of paint.

questions.q: What is the central idea of this text?
choices: Rocks are boring.
Some rocks around the world are amazing, and nature shaped them slowly.
Ireland has columns.
Hikers like cliffs.

questions.q: Which detail SUPPORTS the idea that rocks take a long time to form?
choices: A giant built the columns.
Wind, water, and ice slowly shape stone for millions of years.
Hikers stand on Kjeragbolten.
The Wave is in Arizona.

passages.title: Maya and the Grand Canyon
passages.body:
Maya stared down into the Grand Canyon and her stomach flipped. The trail looked like a tiny brown thread along the cliff. "I can't do this," she whispered. Her older brother, Leo, just smiled. "Yes you can. One step at a time."

The first hour was the hardest. Maya's legs shook, and twice she sat down and almost cried. Each time, Leo waited. He didn't laugh, and he didn't tell her to hurry. He pointed at lizards on the rocks and at hawks circling far below. Slowly, Maya started to look around instead of straight down.

By lunchtime, she had walked farther than she ever had in her life. By afternoon, she was the one pointing things out to Leo. When they finally reached the bottom and stood beside the wide green river, Maya laughed out loud.

That night, in her sleeping bag, she thought about the trail. She had been so afraid at the top. But the canyon hadn't gotten smaller — she had gotten braver. From now on, when something looked too big to do, she would remember: one step at a time.

questions.q: What is the THEME of this story?
choices: Hiking is dangerous.
Big problems can be solved one small step at a time.
Brothers are annoying.
Always bring lunch on a hike.

questions.q: How does Maya CHANGE from the beginning to the end?
choices: She gets more scared.
She becomes braver and more confident.
She gets tired and gives up.
She learns to climb cliffs.

questions.q: Which is the BEST summary of the story?
choices: Maya saw a lizard and a hawk.
Maya was afraid to hike the Grand Canyon, but with her brother's patient help she made it to the bottom and felt proud.
The river at the bottom was green.
Leo did not laugh at his sister.

questions.q: What is the central message the author wants us to remember?
choices: Canyons are deep.
The canyon hadn't gotten smaller — she had gotten braver.
Hawks circle in the sky.
Sleeping bags are warm.

questions.q: What did Leo do that helped Maya the most?
choices: He carried her down.
He waited patiently and pointed out interesting things.
He told her to go faster.
He left her alone on the trail.

passages.title: A Trip to Remember
passages.body:
Monica was so excited! She was visiting her cousin Ben in Arizona, and today they were going to see the Grand Canyon. Monica had never been to the Southwest. She lived in the Red River Valley of Minnesota, where the natural landscape was very different from the Southwest.

Last year, in social studies, Monica's class had studied the Grand Canyon, but she never dreamed that she would get to see it in person. Her parents had decided they wanted to take the family out West and had planned a trip to visit Ben's family in the spring.

Monica hoped to uncover many secrets about the canyon. Her teacher, Ms. Jones, had told the students that Grand Canyon National Park was one of the oldest parks in the United States.

As they drove, Monica wondered if any Native Americans had lived in and near the Grand Canyon. She also recalled her teacher saying that scientists had found artifacts in caves in the canyon. As she watched the bright, sunny desert roll by just outside the car window, Monica wondered if she would find an ancient artifact. She remembered hearing that people used to come to the canyon in search of gold. Those tales proved to be untrue. There was no gold in the canyon.

Ms. Jones had told the class that mules are still used to carry people to the bottom of the canyon and back up. Monica was curious to discover how the mules climbed over the uneven ground. She couldn't wait to get there and unearth the canyon's secrets for herself.

questions.q: Has Monica visited the Grand Canyon before this trip?
choices: Yes, she visits every summer.
No, she had never been to the Southwest.
Yes, she went with her school.
No, but her cousin Ben had visited Minnesota.

questions.q: Why was Monica excited to visit the Grand Canyon?
choices: She wanted to move to Arizona.
She had learned about it in social studies and always wanted to see it.
Her cousin had never been to Minnesota.
She wanted to find gold in the canyon.

questions.q: What did Monica wonder about on the drive?
choices: If she would find an ancient artifact
If her cousin had ever been to Minnesota
If the canyon was smaller than she expected
If her teacher would be there

questions.q: What does the phrase "unearth the canyon's secrets" mean?
choices: Dig up buried treasure in the rocks
Find gold hidden in the canyon
Learn new things about the canyon
Travel through underground caves

questions.q: What evidence in the text supports that the Grand Canyon is historically important?
choices: The desert was bright and sunny.
Scientists have found artifacts in caves in the canyon.
It is located near Minnesota.
Monica's family planned a trip in the spring.

questions.q: What is the central idea of this passage?
choices: Gold was found at the Grand Canyon.
Monica is excited to finally visit the Grand Canyon and discover its secrets.
Mules are used to carry people in Arizona.
Ms. Jones teaches social studies.

passages.title: Thunderstorm Safety
passages.body:
Thunderstorms are dangerous. Every thunderstorm contains lightning, and lightning kills more people each year than tornadoes and hurricanes! Do you know what to do to remain safe during a thunderstorm?

First, go inside a sturdy building or a vehicle and close the windows. Furthermore, the National Weather Service recommends that you stay inside for at least thirty minutes after the last rumble of thunder is heard. Do not use telephones or electrical equipment. Avoid taking showers or baths. If you are unable to get safely inside a building, avoid taking cover beneath tall, isolated trees. Also avoid water, high ground, and metal objects. Knowing what to do (and what not to do) during a thunderstorm could save your life.

questions.q: What is the central idea of this passage?
choices: Lightning is pretty to watch.
There are important things you should do to remain safe during a thunderstorm.
Thunderstorms only happen in summer.
You should always stay outside during storms.

questions.q: What should you do FIRST during a thunderstorm?
choices: Call someone on the phone
Go inside a sturdy building or a vehicle and close the windows
Take a shower or bath
Stand under a tall tree

questions.q: How long does the National Weather Service recommend you stay inside after the last thunder?
choices: Ten minutes
At least thirty minutes
One hour
Five minutes

questions.q: Which of the following should you AVOID during a thunderstorm?
choices: Going inside a sturdy building
Closing the windows
Using telephones or electrical equipment
Staying away from tall trees

questions.q: What kills more people each year than tornadoes and hurricanes?
choices: Floods
Earthquakes
Lightning
Hail

passages.title: The Brave Mouse
passages.body:
¹ A very hungry mouse who said,
"I'll do just what I please!"
Snuck into a house one night
And stole a chunk of cheese.

² He scampered down the passageway
And thinking that was that,
Was very unprepared to see
The shadow of a cat!

³ He dropped the cheese
And turned around,
And headed for his hole
And didn't even think about
The big cheese that he stole.

⁴ When he was finally safe at home
And telling all the mice
He couldn't help but add some girth to
The abandoned slice.

⁵ The kitty, too, became a huge
And formidable beast
To hear the brave mouse tell it
He had nearly been a feast.

⁶ His friends, they all believed him,
For they did not know he lied
And so, the not-so-brave mouse
Was allowed to keep his pride!

questions.q: What kind of attitude does the hungry mouse have at the beginning of the poem?
choices: That he deserves some cheese
That he can do whatever he wants
That he is worried about the cat
That he is braver than the other mice

questions.q: How did the mouse feel when he saw the shadow of a cat?
choices: He felt frightened.
He felt like fighting.
He felt brave.
He felt hungry.

questions.q: Did the mouse ever actually come in contact with, or really see, the cat?
choices: Yes, they fought over the cheese.
No, he only saw the cat's shadow.
Yes, the cat chased him home.
No, but the cat ate the cheese.

questions.q: Why does the mouse exaggerate when he tells the others what happened?
choices: He wants them to admire him.
He wants to explain why he didn't bring back the cheese.
He wants them to think he was brave.
All of the above

questions.q: What are the two rhyming words in stanza 1?
choices: mouse / house
said / night
please / cheese
one / chunk

passages.title: Lights in the Deep Sea
passages.body:
Imagine diving thousands of feet below the ocean's surface, far beyond where sunlight can reach. Down here, in what scientists call the midnight zone, the water is pitch black and near freezing. Yet this dark wilderness is anything but lifeless — it is home to some of the most remarkable creatures on Earth.

Many deep-sea animals produce their own light through a chemical process called bioluminescence. The anglerfish, for example, dangles a glowing lure from its forehead to attract smaller fish right into its jaws. Jellyfish pulse with ghostly blue and green flashes to startle predators. Tiny shrimp-like krill glow along their bellies so that, when seen from below, they blend in with the faint light filtering down from above — a clever trick known as counter-illumination.

Why did so many unrelated species develop the ability to glow? Scientists believe the answer lies in survival. In a world without sunlight, light becomes a powerful tool. Some animals use it to hunt, others to hide, and still others to communicate with mates across the vast darkness.

Recent expeditions using remotely operated vehicles have revealed that bioluminescence is not rare at all. In fact, researchers estimate that up to ninety percent of creatures in the deep ocean can produce light. What once seemed like a strange exception turns out to be the rule. The deep sea glows, and we are only beginning to understand why.

questions.q: What is the central idea of this passage?
choices: The ocean is very deep and cold.
Bioluminescence is widespread in the deep sea and is essential for survival.
Anglerfish are dangerous predators.
Scientists use submarines to explore.

questions.q: How is this passage mainly organized?
choices: In time order from past to present
By comparing the deep sea to outer space
By explaining what bioluminescence is, giving examples, and then explaining why it exists
By listing problems and their solutions

questions.q: Based on context clues, what does the word "counter-illumination" most likely mean?
choices: Shining a light to count objects in the dark
Using light on the body to match the surrounding brightness and avoid being seen
A type of lamp used on submarines
Turning off all light to become invisible

questions.q: Which detail BEST supports the idea that bioluminescence helps animals survive?
choices: The midnight zone is near freezing.
The anglerfish dangles a glowing lure to attract prey into its jaws.
Researchers use remotely operated vehicles.
Up to ninety percent of deep-sea creatures glow.

questions.q: What does the author mean by "What once seemed like a strange exception turns out to be the rule"?
choices: There are rules against studying the deep sea.
Scientists used to think bioluminescence was rare, but now know it is extremely common.
Glowing animals are breaking the laws of nature.
Only exceptional animals can produce light.

questions.q: According to the passage, approximately what percentage of deep-sea creatures can produce light?
choices: About ten percent
About fifty percent
Up to ninety percent
One hundred percent

passages.title: The Oak and the Reed
passages.body:
¹ A mighty oak stood tall and proud
Beside a winding stream,
Its trunk as thick as castle walls,
Its crown a leafy dream.

² Beside it grew a slender reed
That swayed with every breeze.
The oak looked down and laughed aloud,
"You bow to winds with ease!"

³ "How pitiful you are," it said,
"To bend at every gust.
I stand unbowed through every storm —
My strength is what I trust."

⁴ The reed just smiled and softly said,
"Perhaps you're right, old friend.
But I have learned a simple truth:
The wise know when to bend."

⁵ That night a furious tempest came
With thunder, rain, and hail.
The wind roared like a thousand drums
And sang a howling tale.

⁶ The oak held firm, refused to sway,
And fought the raging gale.
But crack! — its mighty trunk gave way
And crashed into the vale.

⁷ Come morning, when the sun returned
And birds began to sing,
The little reed stood straight once more
And danced with early spring.

questions.q: What is the THEME of this poem?
choices: Big trees are stronger than small plants.
Being flexible and humble can be wiser than being rigid and proud.
Storms are dangerous and should be avoided.
Reeds are better than oak trees.

questions.q: Which line contains a SIMILE?
choices: Its crown a leafy dream
The wind roared like a thousand drums
That night a furious tempest came
The little reed stood straight once more

questions.q: "The wind roared" and "sang a howling tale" are examples of —
choices: simile
personification
hyperbole
alliteration

questions.q: What happens to the oak tree during the storm?
choices: It bends gracefully in the wind.
Its trunk cracks and it crashes to the ground.
It loses a few leaves but survives.
It is struck by lightning.

questions.q: What does the reed mean when it says "The wise know when to bend"?
choices: Smart people exercise every day.
It is wise to be flexible and adapt rather than stubbornly resist.
Bending down is a sign of respect.
Reeds are the wisest plants in the forest.

questions.q: Which pair of words rhyme in stanza 5?
choices: came / hail
drums / tale
hail / tale
night / rain
